# RestClient

Promise based HTTP client for NodeJS and the browser, extending [Axios](https://github.com/axios/axios).

## :warning: Nordic 9 Warning

> [!WARNING]
>
> **The current version of master is only compatible with Nordic 9. If you are using Nordic 8, please use the previous version of the repository.**
>
> Use [v4.x.x](https://github.com/melisource/fury_frontend-restclient/tree/v4.x.x) for modules compatible with Nordic 8.

## Installation

```sh
npm install frontend-restclient
```

## Usage

```js
import { RestClient } from 'frontend-restclient';

/**
 * Create a new RestClient with a custom config
 */
const request = RestClient({
  meliDomain: 'site-domains',
  // Resolves to:
  //   Fury/Sandbox:  http://site-domains.melisystems.com
  //   Office/CI:     https://site-domains.melioffice.com
  timeout: 5000,
});

export function fetchData(req, res, next) {
  request
    .get('/site_domains', {
      params: {
        id: 'mercadolibre.com.ar',
      },
    })
    .then((response) => {
      res.locals.data = response.data;
      /*
      {
        "id": "mercadolibre.com.ar",
        "site_id": "MLA",
        "country_id": "AR",
        "locale": "es_AR",
        "tag": "ML"
      }
      */
      next();
    })
    .catch((err) => next(err));
}
```

## API

The RestClient uses Axios internally, so all Axios configurations are available. Refer to the [Axios Documentation](https://github.com/axios/axios?tab=readme-ov-file#request-config) to see all configurations and default values.

Aside from Axios default configurations, the RestClient has some extra configurations or modifies Axios default values, so only those will be documented below.

### Instance Configuration

```js
const request = RestClient(config); // Instance config (used in all request).
```

| Property              | Type       | Environment | Description                                                                                                                                                                      |
| --------------------- | ---------- | ----------- | -------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- |
| `meliDomain`          | `string`   | Node        | Meli internal subdomain. Builds `baseURL` based on `env.FURY` or `env.SANDBOX`; CI uses dev domain. See [meliDomain](#melidomain-only-for-node).                                 |
| `allowAbsoluteUrls`   | `boolean`  | Node        | Allow absolute URLs to override configured `baseURL`. If set to `false` and an absolute URL is used, no request is made and an error is thrown.                                  |
| `allowRepeatedParams` | `boolean`  | Node        | Indicates if repeated parameters are allowed in the query string without throwing an error.                                                                                      |
| `logErrors`           | `boolean`  | Node        | Indicates if errors should be logged.                                                                                                                                            |
| `sendCSRFToken`       | `boolean`  | Browser     | Specify whether to set the `x-csrf-token` header with the CSRF token.                                                                                                            |
| `retry`               | `Object`   | Node        | Setup request retry attempts. Refer to the [Retry Section](#retry-strategies-only-for-node).                                                                                     |
| `cache`               | `Object`   | Node        | Cache configuration to use with default in-memory cache. Refer to the [Cache Section](#cache-only-for-node).                                                                     |
| `cacheClient`         | `Function` | Node        | Pass a Cache client initialization function. Refer to the [Cache Client Section](#custom-cache-client).                                                                          |
| `eTagCache`           | `Object`   | Node        | Specifies if an in-memory cache should be used for ETag header caching. Refer to the [ETag Cache Section](#etag-cache).                                                          |
| `requestHook`         | `Function` | Node        | Function that will be executed before the requests are made, allowing to modify the request config. Refer to the [Request Hook Section](#request-hook-only-for-node).            |
| `responseHook`        | `Function` | Node        | Function that will be executed right after the response is returned, allowing to modify the response object. Refer to the [Response Hook Section](#response-hook-only-for-node). |

> [!TIP]
>
> To know which are the default values, refer to each one:
>
> - [Node default values](https://github.com/melisource/fury_frontend-restclient/blob/master/config/defaults.js#L22)
> - [Browser defaults values](https://github.com/melisource/fury_frontend-restclient/blob/master/config/defaults.browser.js#L15).

### meliDomain (only for Node)

Builds the `baseURL` automatically from a Meli internal subdomain, based on the runtime environment:

- On Fury or Sandbox infrastructure → `http://<meliDomain>.melisystems.com`
- Outside Fury/Sandbox, or in CI → `https://<meliDomain>.melioffice.com`

```js
const client = RestClient({ meliDomain: 'site-domains' });
```

`meliDomain` must be a valid Meli subdomain name. `meliDomain` and `baseURL` are mutually exclusive — passing both throws an error.

> [!NOTE]
> **CI behaviour:** When inside a CI job the development domain (`https://<meliDomain>.melioffice.com`) is used.

When `meliDomain` is not set, the default Node `baseURL` follows the same environment check:

- On Fury or Sandbox infrastructure → `http://internal.mercadolibre.com`
- Outside Fury/Sandbox, or in CI → `https://internal-api.mercadolibre.com`

### Request Configuration

When making a request, a config object can be passed as a parameter with the same options as when instantiating the RestClient, with the exception of the following properties which can't be overridden:

- `baseURL`
- `allowAbsoluteUrls`
- `allowRepeatedParams`
- `cacheClient`

```js
const request = RestClient();
request.get(url, config); // Request config.
```

The following properties are also accepted or modified.

| Property    | Type      | Environment | Description                                                                                                                                                                                   |
| ----------- | --------- | ----------- | --------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- |
| `cache`     | `boolean` | Node        | Indicates that the response should be retrieved from cache instead of network                                                                                                                 |
| `eTagCache` | `boolean` | Node        | Indicates that the client should cache responses with the `ETag` header, send the `If-None-Match` header with cached etag on requests, and use cached content on responses with 304 statuses. |
| `context`   | `Object`  | Node        | Used to set headers like `X-Request-Id` to trace HTTP requests across Meli infrastructure. Refer to the [Context Section](#context-only-for-node).                                            |

### Supported methods

- RestClient#get(url [, config])
- RestClient#delete(url [, config])
- RestClient#head(url [, config])
- RestClient#post(url [, config])
- RestClient#put(url[, config])
- RestClient#patch(url[, config])

Where:

- `url` is the server URL that will be used for the request. Only the `url` is required.
- `config` is optional. These are the available `config` only to use in requests:

## Response schema

The response for a request contains the following information:

```json
{
  // `data` is the response that was provided by the server
  "data": {},

  // `status` is the HTTP status code from the server response
  "status": 200,

  // `statusText` is the HTTP status message from the server response
  "statusText": "OK",

  // `headers` the headers that the server responded with
  "headers": {},

  // `config` is the config that was provided for the request
  "config": {},

  // `retries` is the quantity of retries done to achieve a successful response
  "retries": 0
}
```

## Handling Errors

The default behavior is to reject any response with a status code outside the 2xx range and treat it as an error.

Refer to the [Axios documentation](https://github.com/axios/axios?tab=readme-ov-file#error-types) to know all possible error types and their causes.

```js
request.get('/site_domains/50').catch((error) => {
  if (error.response) {
    // The request was made and the server responded with a status code
    // that falls out of the range of 2xx
    console.log(error.response.data);
    console.log(error.response.status);
    console.log(error.response.headers);
  } else if (error.request) {
    // The request was made but no response was received
    console.log(error.request);
  } else {
    // Something happened in setting up the request that triggered an Error
    console.log('Error', error.message);
  }
  console.log(error.config);
});
```

Using the `validateStatus` config option, you can override the default condition (status >= 200 && status < 300) and define HTTP code(s) that should throw an error.

```js
request.get('/site_domains/50', {
  validateStatus: function (status) {
    return status < 500; // Resolve only if the status code is less than 500
  },
});
```

Using `.toJSON()` you get an object with more information about the HTTP error.

```js
request.get('/site_domains/50').catch((error) => {
  console.log(error.toJSON());
});
```

## Request hook (only for node)

The request hook is a function that will be executed before the requests are made. To use a request hook, you just need to pass your function in the config object, for example:

```js
const request = RestClient({
  meliDomain: 'site-domains',
  requestHook: (requestConfig) => {
    console.log(
      'This is your request hook being executed. The config object is:',
      requestConfig,
    );
  },
});
```

## Response hook (only for node)

The response hook is a function that will be executed right after the response is returned. To use a response hook, you just need to pass your function in the config object, for example:

```js
const request = RestClient({
  meliDomain: 'site-domains',
  responseHook: (response) => {
    console.log(
      'This is your response hook being executed. The response object is:',
      response,
    );
  },
});
```

## Context (only for Node)

The `context` is used internally by the RestClient to set headers like `X-Request-Id` to trace HTTP requests across Meli infrastructure.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Setting a context is mandatory to make requests. If no context is found, your app will get traceability errors in Fury.

### Context - Automatic Context

Context can be automatically calculated by the RestClient if there's a Server Context present.

To have a Server Context, you need to be in one of the following cases:

- Use Nordic 9+
- Use [`@meli/express-server`](https://github.com/melisource/fury_node-core-libs/tree/master/packages/express-server#meliexpress-server) as a Node server

### Context - Manual Context

If you can't use the automatic context feature, you can manually create and pass the context as an argument.

> [!NOTE]
> Passing a context manually will override the automatic context if present.

#### Manual Context - Propagate Request Context

The `buildContext(req)` should be used in Express applications to create a new `context` from the `req` object.

```js
import { RestClient, buildContext } from 'frontend-restclient';

const request = RestClient();

export function fetchData(req, res, next) {
  request
    .get('/site_domains/50', {
      context: buildContext(req), // `req` is the request object available in Express.
    })
    .then((response) => {
      res.locals.data = response.data;
      next();
    })
    .catch((err) => next(err));
}
```

#### Manual Context - Create New Context

The `buildFlowStarterContext()` function should be used in internal libraries/modules that make requests which do not originate from an incoming request to the web server.

```js
import { RestClient, buildFlowStarterContext } from 'frontend-restclient';

const request = RestClient();

request.get('/site_domains/50', {
  context: buildFlowStarterContext();
});
```

### Troubleshooting: Inspection of the request context

Sometimes, it may be necessary to inspect the request context in order to understand the headers being sent to a given endpoint. In these cases, you can use the `LOG_LEVEL` environment variable set to `debug` to enable additional logging for your request.

- In the package.json file, update the start script to the following:

```json
  "start": "LOG_LEVEL=debug NODE_ENV=production node ./index.js"
```

This is meant to be used in the Fury environment, since the sidecar that injects some headers necessary for the creation of the context, doesn't exist in development environments.

## Retry Strategies (only for Node)

The RestClient provides retry strategies that allow you to define the time interval between each attempt.

> [!NOTE]
> By default, the simple retry strategy is used alongside the `TransportRetryPolicy`. If you want to turn it off, just pass `{ retry: false }`.

You can use two basic strategies:

### Simple Retry

It will just retry for a fixed number of times and wait for a fixed interval between runs:

```js
const request = RestClient();

request.get('/site_domains/50', {
  retry: {
    maxRetries: 3, // The maximum amount of times to retry. Default: 1.
    delay: 500, // The number of milliseconds to wait before starting each retry. Default: 0.
  },
});
```

### Exponential Backoff Retry

It will increase wait time between retries between a delay and a max delay value, growing exponentially between runs:

```js
const request = RestClient();

request.get('/site_domains/50', {
  retry: {
    delay: 500, // The number of milliseconds before starting the first retry.
    maxDelay: 5000, // The maximum number of milliseconds between two retries.
    factor: 2, // The exponential factor to use.
  },
});
```

### Turning off retry

```js
const request = RestClient({
  retry: {
    maxRetries: 5,
  },
});

// It won't execute any retries:
request.get('/site_domains/50', { retry: false });
```

You can also disable retries using the instance config:

```js
const request = RestClient({
  retry: false,
});

// It won't execute any retries:
request.get('/site_domains/50');
```

## Retry policies (only for Node)

The retry policies establish when a request should be retried. The RestClient provides many retry policies; by default, the `TransportRetryPolicy` is used.

### Policies

#### ServerErrorPolicy

Only for GET, HEAD, and OPTIONS request methods. Makes a retry only if error status is >= 500.

#### IdempotentTimeoutPolicy

Only for GET, HEAD, and OPTIONS request methods. Makes a retry only if the connection or read timeouts are exceeded.

#### NonIdempotentPolicy

Only for POST, LOCK, PATCH and DELETE request methods. Makes a retry only if the connection or read timeouts are exceeded.

#### TransportRetryPolicy

Default retry policy. Combines IdempotentTimeoutPolicy and ServerErrorPolicy.

#### CustomRetryPolicy

This feature allows you to define your own retry policy. Take a look at the [usage](#customretrypolicy) examples.

#### Policies Usage

To use any of the supported policies, you need to import the `retryPolicies`.

```js
import { RestClient, retryPolicies } from 'frontend-restclient';

const request = RestClient({
  retry: {
    maxRetries: 5,
    delay: 500,
    policy: retryPolicies.ServerError, // Set the default policy as `ServerError`
  },
});

// If a server error occurs (status >= 500), the RestClient will retry up to 5 times
request.get('/site_domains/50');
```

You can also overwrite the policy established on the instance config:

```js
const request = RestClient({
  retry: {
    maxRetries: 5,
    delay: 500,
    policy: retryPolicies.ServerError, // Set the default policy as `ServerError`
  },
});

// It will retry only on server errors since it's using instance config
request.get('/site_domains/50');

// It will only retry on timeouts
request.get('/site_domains/50', {
  retry: {
    // Set the current request policy as `IdempotentTimeoutPolicy`
    policy: retryPolicies.IdempotentTimeoutPolicy,
  },
});
```

### Implementing a custom retry policy

To implement a custom retry policy, you have to create a class that fulfills these requirements:

- Extends from `RetryPolicy`
- Implement the `hasToRetry` method (this method should return a truthy or falsy value that will specify whether to retry or not)

```js
import { RestClient, retryPolicies } from 'frontend-restclient';

class MyCustomRetryPolicy extends retryPolicies.RetryPolicy {
  static hasToRetry(error) {
    // Here goes all your logic
    // Let's suppose we want a mix of ServerError and NonIdempotent:
    return retryPolicies.ServerError || retryPolicies.NonIdempotentPolicy;
  }
}

// Now we just need to use it
const request = RestClient({
  retry: {
    maxRetries: 5,
    delay: 500,
    policy: MyCustomRetryPolicy, // Set the default policy as `ServerError`
  },
});

// It will retry on server errors or on non-idempotent requests
request.get('/site_domains/50');
```

## Pool and Agents (only for Node)

In Node, an Agent is responsible for managing connection persistence and reuse for HTTP clients.

The HTTP Agent is used for pooling sockets used in HTTP client requests. You can configure this using the `httpAgent` and `httpsAgent` to define a custom agent to be used when performing HTTP and HTTPS requests.

You can read more about how pooling works in Node in the [http.Agent documentation](https://nodejs.org/api/http.html#http_class_http_agent).

```js
import http from 'node:http';
import https from 'node:https';
import { RestClient } from 'frontend-restclient';

/**
 * Create an instance with custom agents
 */
const request = RestClient({
  httpAgent: new http.Agent({
    keepAlive: true,
    keepAliveMsecs: 1000,
    maxSockets: 100,
    maxFreeSockets: 50,
  }),

  httpsAgent: new https.Agent({
    keepAlive: true,
    keepAliveMsecs: 2000,
    maxSockets: 100,
    maxFreeSockets: 60,
  }),
});
```

Also, you can use a different HTTP agent for a particular request:

```js
request.get('/particular', {
  httpAgent: new http.Agent({
    keepAlive: true,
    keepAliveMsecs: 1000,
    maxSockets: 100,
    maxFreeSockets: 50,
  }),

  httpsAgent: new https.Agent({
    keepAlive: true,
    keepAliveMsecs: 2000,
    maxSockets: 100,
    maxFreeSockets: 60,
  }),
});
```

## Downloading data to stream (only for Node)

To download data to stream, just configure the header `accept-encoding: ''` and the `responseType: 'stream'` to make your request.

```js
import { RestClient } from 'frontend-restclient';

const request = RestClient();

const requestHandler = (req, res) => {
  request
    .get('https://mla-s1-p.mlstatic.com/700515-MLA25252109535_122016-M.jpg', {
      headers: {
        'accept-encoding': '', // disable gzip
      },
      responseType: 'stream',
    })
    .then((response) => {
      // Pipe to Express' Response
      res.append('content-type', response.headers['content-type']);
      res.append(
        'content-disposition',
        response.headers['content-disposition'],
      );
      response.data.pipe(res);
    });
};
```

## Uploading multipart data

To upload multipart data, you should use the [`FormData`](https://nodejs.org/api/globals.html#class-formdata) class and make your request.

To send the data as a `multipart/formdata`, you need to pass a `FormData` instance as a payload. Setting the `Content-Type` header is not required as the RestClient guesses it based on the payload type.

```js
import { RestClient } from 'frontend-restclient';

const request = RestClient();

/**
 * Create a FormData
 */
const form = new FormData();
form.append('my_field', 'my value');

request.post('/multipart-form', { data: form }).then((response) => {
  console.log('done');
});
```

## Interceptors (only for Node)

You can intercept requests or responses before they are handled by then or catch.

```js
import { RestClient } from 'frontend-restclient';

const request = RestClient();

// Add a request interceptor
request.client.interceptors.request.use(
  (config) => {
    // Do something before request is sent
    return config;
  },
  (error) => {
    // Do something with request error
    return Promise.reject(error);
  },
);

// Add a response interceptor
request.client.interceptors.response.use(
  (response) => {
    // Do something with response data
    return response;
  },
  (error) => {
    // Do something with response error
    return Promise.reject(error);
  },
);
```

## Cache (only for Node)

By default, the RestClient won't use any cache. There are two different types of cache:

- `Control-Cache`, which by default uses an in-memory cache, but any [Meli Toolkit Cache Client](https://github.com/melisource/fury_node-melitk-cache) can be used.
- `ETag` cache.

### Control-Cache

If you need to use `Control-Cache`, then you can use the [built-in in-memory cache](#in-memory---stale-lru-cache-default), or pass [your own cache client](#custom-cache-client), usually a Fury Cache Service client.

### Shared Interface

Each cache must have these defined methods:

- `get(key)`
- `set(key, value, maxAgeHeader)`
- `delete(key)`

#### Keys

Keys are composed as follows:

`${HTTPMethod}-${url}-${stringifiedParams}`

Since this information oftentimes exceeds the 250 chars restraint keys have, each key **is hashed with md5**.

### In Memory - Stale LRU Cache (default)

This in-memory cache is based on [stale-lru-cache](https://github.com/cyberthom/stale-lru-cache) and integrated into the RestClient. The cache will only be applied to `GET` methods using the value of the header `cache-control` or a custom `maxAge`. For more information about the cache-control header, refer to [cache-control](#cache-control-with-the-in-memory-cache).

To use it, you can just enable it on the specific call you want to be cached:

```js
request.get('/site_domains/50', { cache: true });
```

> [!NOTE]
> The cache is disabled by default.

#### Cache-Control with the In-Memory Cache

The `cache-control` directives present in the response will always be respected for the default in-memory cache.

For more information about `cache-control`, you can look at the [Cache-Control RFC](https://www.rfc-editor.org/rfc/rfc9111#name-cache-control), [this RFC on Stale Content](https://tools.ietf.org/html/rfc5861), or the [MDN Docs](https://developer.mozilla.org/docs/Web/HTTP/Headers/Cache-Control).

##### Custom Cache config for each request

If no `cache-control` header is present in the response, cache behaviour can be overridden for each request:

- `maxSize`: Maximum cache size (default: 500).
- `maxAge`: Time in seconds after which items will expire (default: Infinity).
- `staleWhileRevalidate`: Time in seconds, after maxAge has expired, when items are marked as stale but still usable (default: 0).

`cache-control` header **ALWAYS** takes precedence.

```js
request
  .get('/site_domains/50', {
    cache: {
      maxAge: 600, // cache for 10 minutes
    },
  })
  .then((response) => {
    console.log(response.data);
  });
```

### Custom Cache Client

You can use any of the provided cache clients by the Fury Cache Service. Please refer to [the docs](https://furydocs.io/node-melitk-cache/latest/guide/#/) for more info.

```js
import { RestClient } from 'frontend-restclient';
import { CacheClient } from 'node-melitk-cache';

const request = RestClient({
  cacheClient: () => new CacheClient(cacheConfiguration),
});
```

To use it you can just enable it on the specific call you want to be cached as follows:

```js
request.get('/site_domains/50', { cache: true });
```

Note that it will use the `max-age` defined in the request `cache-control` header if present.

If you want to use a specific `maxAge` and no `cache-control` header is present:

```js
request.get('/site_domains/50', {
  cache: {
    maxAge: 10000, // Time in seconds
  },
});
```

## ETag Cache

The [`ETag` header](https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Web/HTTP/Headers/ETag) can be used to avoid unnecessary traffic over a network when requesting [unchanged resources](https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Web/HTTP/Headers/ETag#Caching_of_unchanged_resources). It will always use an in-memory cache; otherwise, it will generate traffic to other endpoints, losing the advantages of this technique.

In order to use it, you have to pass the `eTagCache` configuration with a truthy value:

```js
import { RestClient } from 'frontend-restclient';

const request = RestClient({
  eTagCache: true,
});
```

### Memory consumption

As it uses an in-memory cache, make sure you use it with non-dynamic calls such as static assets. Using it with dynamic calls (e.g., calls that depend on the user, item, etc.) can lead the application to consume too much RAM because of unnecessary cached responses.

There's an option to pass a configuration object on the `eTagCache` configuration. If you want to use it with dynamically generated URLs, make sure to set a limit by setting a `maxSize` property on the configuration object, that will be passed to our in-memory cache (Stale LRU Cache):

```js
import { RestClient } from 'frontend-restclient';

const request = RestClient({
  eTagCache: {
    maxSize: 100,
  },
});
```

## Cancellation

Use an `AbortController` to cancel requests.

```js
import { RestClient } from 'frontend-restclient';

const request = RestClient();
const controller = new AbortController();

request
  .get('/user/12345', {
    signal: controller.signal,
  })
  .catch((error) => {
    if (error.code === 'ERR_CANCELED') {
      console.log('Request canceled', error.message);
    } else {
      // handle error
    }
  });

// Cancel request
controller.abort();
```

> [!NOTE]
>
> You can cancel several requests with the same `AbortController`.
> If a controller is already aborted at the moment of starting a request, then the request is cancelled immediately, without any attempts to make a real request.

## Automatic Redirection (browser only)

Sometimes user redirection is needed to provide the best experience for our users, like redirecting an unauthenticated user to the login page, but this is not possible for native AJAX requests.

To cover these cases and prevent apps from handling them manually, the RestClient will automatically redirect users to the URL provided in the `Location` response header when:

- The `Location` response header is present
- The response status code is either `401`, `402`, or `403`

In these cases, the Request Promise will not reject and will redirect the user by calling `window.location.assign()`.

## Avoid sending CSRF Token Header

In some cases (for example, a cross-origin request), you might not want to send the header `x-csrf-token` inside a specific request or all of them (disabling the header for all requests may be considered as a bad practice from the security point of view).

In order to do this, you should use the config flag `sendCSRFToken` when you create your RestClient instance or for a specific request. _The default value for this flag is `true`._

### Request level turn off

```js
import { RestClient } from 'frontend-restclient';

const request = RestClient();

request.post('/users', {
  sendCSRFToken: false,
});
```

### Instance level turn off

```js
import { RestClient } from 'frontend-restclient';

const request = RestClient({
  sendCSRFToken: false,
});

request.post('/users');
```
